data.title: Westlake Chemical Partners names Jonathan Baksht CFO
data.description:
• Westlake Chemical Partners (WKLP) on Monday appointed Jonathan Baksht its chief financial officer, effective June 15.  
  • Baksht replaces M. Steven Bender, who will retire by the end of the year. 
  • Baksht most recently served as executive vice president and CFO of Fortune Brands Innovations (FBIN). He previously held CFO roles at Pactiv Evergreen, now Novolex, and Valaris (VAL). 
  • “We are pleased to have an executive with Jon’s depth of experience from the oil and gas, packaging and building products industries join our management team,” said CEO Jean-Marc Gilson.
  • Baksht will also join the board of Westlake Chemical Partners.

data.title: RTX's Raytheon wins $213M contract modification for Zumwalt-class destroyer
data.description:
• RTX's Raytheon Missiles & Defense (RTX) got a $213.4M modification to a previously awarded U.S. Navy contract for mission systems work on the Zumwalt-class destroyer program, the Department of Defense said on Monday. 
  • The modification to the contract covers combat system installation, integration, development, testing, maintenance, corrections, and modernization for Zumwalt-class mission systems and related equipment.
  • Work will be performed majorly in Portsmouth and Tewksbury and also in Pascagoula, Nashua, San Diego, and Fort Wayne. The contract is expected to be completed by the end of the current fiscal year.
  • The Zumwalt-class destroyer is a class of three United States Navy guided-missile destroyers designed as multi-mission stealth ships with a focus on land attack. 
  • The contract was issued by Naval Sea Systems Command in Washington.

data.title: Apple's Tim Cook leaving as CEO may be seen as 'mixed,' change in 'strategy' by investors: analysts
data.description:
Apple's (AAPL) Tim Cook stepping down as CEO and hardware chief John Ternus succeeding him at the helm saw strong reactions from analysts. 

SA Analysts

"Tim Cook brought to Apple exactly what was expected of him - operational excellence (as a former COO under Jobs), although little innovation. In my latest Apple breakdown, I said the company’s miss on AI wasn’t a genius master plan, but the simple inability to innovate and achieve tangible results with Apple Intelligence. I assume it was hard for the board to accept the reality of Apple falling behind in the AI race, so the choice of the new CEO seems perfect for the goal of continuing innovation and growing excellence," said Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian.

Cook, who has been CEO for 15 years, will step down from the role and become executive chairman, effective Sept. 1. On Ternus' appointment, Cook said that “John Ternus has the mind of an engineer, the soul of an innovator, and the heart to lead with integrity and with honor. He is a visionary whose contributions to Apple over 25 years are already too numerous to count, and he is without question the right person to lead Apple into the future."

Ostian noted that Cook's quote about Ternus shows that the focus is shifting to innovation and future vision. "I assume Apple will be jumping into the AI race, possibly with some new ideas and ways to implement the Agentic AI, or even create their own solutions. At least, this is what I believe the board expects from the new CEO," Ostian added.

Seeking Alpha analyst Julian Lin, Investing Group Leader for Best Of Breed Growth Stocks, said that "I expect investors to view this change at the top as a possibility of (but not guarantee) a change in the typical “don’t rock the boat” strategy of the predecessor. While AAPL has performed phenomenally well in exercising the “second mover advantage,” its delays in pursuing AI might not lead to the same results. Time will tell if the incoming CEO may seek to change the narrative."

Wall Street Analysts

Wedbush said Tim Cook leaving as CEO in a "shocker headline," while maintaining its Outperform rating and $350 price target on the stock.

"Investors will view this as mixed as this was a sudden move to Executive Chairman as there was clearly a push for change at the C-Suite. These will be big shoes to fill and the timing of Cook exiting stage left as CEO could make sense but also creates questions. Apple is making a major transition on its AI strategy and longtime CEO and legendary Cook leaving now is a surprise. There was growing pressure on Apple to produce a successful AI strategy and Cook must feel that the pieces are now in place heading into WWDC [Worldwide Developers Conference] to hand over the reigns at this time," said analysts led by Dan Ives.

The analysts noted that Cook leaves a lasting legacy in Cupertino, and there will be a lot of pressure on Ternus to produce success out of the gates, especially on the AI front. While there were rumors of Cook leaving as CEO, investors will, for now, have more questions than answers around the timing and what this means for the broader Apple strategy, the analysts added.

"This will put even more pressure on Apple to produce success and its product roadmap at WWDC with AI front and center," said Ives and his team.

Apple also appointed Johny Srouji as chief hardware officer. Srouji, who served as senior vice president of hardware technologies, will now assume an expanded role leading hardware engineering, which Ternus recently oversaw, as well as the hardware technologies organization.

Arthur Levinson, who had been the non-executive chairman at Apple for the past 15 years, will become its lead independent director, effective Sept. 1. Ternus will also become a board member, effective Sept. 1.

Shares of Apple dipped about 1% premarket on Tuesday.

data.title: Mercantile Bank Non-GAAP EPS of $1.46 beats by $0.15, revenue of $67.6M beats by $0.19M
data.description:
• Mercantile Bank press release (MBWM): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.46 beats by $0.15.
  • Revenue of $67.6M (+18.1% Y/Y) beats by $0.19M.
  • Reported GAAP net income of $22.7 million, or $1.32 per diluted share, for the first quarter of 2026.
  • Return on average assets of 1.4 percent and return on average equity of 12.5 percent, and 1.5 percent and 14.0 percent, respectively, on a non-GAAP basis
  • Tangible book value per common share of $37.34 as of March 31, 2026, up $0.56, or approximately 2 percent, since December 31, 2025, and $2.92, or over 8 percent, since March 31, 2025
  • Net revenue expansion of over 18 percent compared to the prior-year first quarter, including net interest income growth of over 15 percent
  • Increased net interest margin primarily reflecting lower cost of funds and continued repricing of matured fixed rate loans and securities

data.title: Biggest stock movers Tuesday: AMZN, LCID, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched up Tuesday morning while oil, gold, and silver slipped, with investors cautiously eyeing U.S.-Iran peace talks prospects as a temporary ceasefire deadline approaches.

Here are some of Tuesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Amazon.com (AMZN) +3% - Shares gained after announcing a deeper AI push with Anthropic, committing an additional $5B investment with plans to scale up to $20B over time tied to milestones. The expanded partnership cements AWS as Anthropic’s primary cloud and training provider. The AI firm is set to spend over $100B on AWS infrastructure over the next decade, including Trainium chips and Graviton compute, alongside securing up to 5GW of capacity. The deal also integrates Anthropic’s Claude platform directly into AWS, strengthening Amazon’s position in the generative AI race and driving investor optimism.
  • Lucid Group (LCID) +2% - Shares rose after Uber Technologies ((UBER)) disclosed an 11.5% passive stake (~37.75M shares) via its subsidiary, alongside deeper strategic ties. The investment is part of Lucid’s broader $1.05B capital raise, which includes backing from the Public Investment Fund. Uber also boosted its commitment to purchase at least 35,000 Lucid vehicles for its planned global robotaxi network and increased its total investment in the EV maker to $500M, reinforcing a long-term partnership in autonomous mobility.

Biggest stock losers

  • AXT (AXTI) -8% - Shares dipped after announcing a public stock offering, including a 15% overallotment option. Proceeds are largely earmarked to expand indium phosphide substrate production at its Beijing Tongmei unit, alongside funding R&D and general corporate needs, raising dilution concerns despite the growth investment angle.
  • Alaska Air Group (ALK) -3% - Shares slid despite strong Q1 results after suspending its FY2026 outlook due to limited earnings visibility driven by fuel price volatility and geopolitical uncertainty. The airline flagged a wide range of potential outcomes for Q2, opting to withhold EPS guidance while indicating capacity growth of ~1% and high single-digit unit revenue gains, though partially offset by weather-related disruptions in Hawai’i. Rising fuel costs, expected to average ~$4.50 per gallon, are projected to add ~$600M in expenses (~$3.60 EPS headwind), while near-term unit cost pressures from capacity adjustments, training, and one-off items are expected to ease in the second half of the year.

data.title: European equities gain cautiously as investors eye U.S. Iran talks
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.11% to 10,620. UK's unemployment rate drops to 4.9% in February. The sterling pound held near $1.35, slightly below last week’s three-week peak, as political uncertainty and geopolitical risks dominated markets.

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.51% to 24,542.

France (CAC:IND) -0.03% to 8,328. 

In other parts of Europe, Spain's trade deficit fell in February.

Producer prices in Poland fell 0.8% Y/Y in March.

Switzerland’s trade surplus narrowed to CHF 2.7B in March.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) traded 0.05% higher to 621.7, as developments in the Middle East continue to dominate headlines and traders look for fresh catalysts. Reports indicate that Iran will join the U.S. for talks in Islamabad. The investors will be closely watching whether the ceasefire, which is set to expire Wednesday evening, will be extended.

On the corporate front, earnings are due from Thales, Associated British Foods, and Beiersdorf, among others.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury was up 1 basis point to 4.26%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 4.85%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 1 basis point to 2.97%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Who is Apple’s next CEO, John Ternus?
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) has named John Ternus as its next CEO, succeeding Tim Cook. Ternus is a longtime Apple executive and current head of hardware engineering.

Ternus joined Apple in 2001 and has spent more than two decades rising through the ranks of the company’s product organization. He currently serves as senior vice president of hardware engineering, overseeing development of key products including the iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple Watch and AirPods.

Before taking charge of Apple’s broader hardware division, Ternus played a central role in the engineering of several flagship devices, helping lead transitions across Mac products and major iPhone generations. He became a more visible public figure inside Apple during product launch events lately, where he presented new Macs and iPads.

Ternus comes from Apple’s product engineering side. That could signal a stronger focus on hardware innovation as the company navigates slowing smartphone growth, increasing AI competition, and efforts to expand newer categories such as wearables and mixed reality.

Ternus is widely viewed as a steady internal successor who understands Apple’s culture, product development process, and tightly integrated ecosystem. His appointment suggests Apple favored continuity over an external shake-up.

As incoming CEO, Ternus will face major challenges, including accelerating Apple’s artificial intelligence strategy, managing regulatory scrutiny, sustaining iPhone demand, and finding the company’s next major growth engine beyond smartphones.

For investors, the leadership change may initially be considered stable and low-risk, but attention is likely to shift quickly toward how Ternus plans to shape Apple’s next phase of growth.

data.title: Spain's trade deficit fell in February
data.description:
• Spain's trade deficit fell to €3.3B in February 2026 from €3.4B in the same month of 2025, as exports decreased less than imports.
  • Exports fell by 0.8% Y/Y to €31.7B, amid lower shipments of energy products (-21.8%); chemical products (-9.2%) and durable consumer goods (-6.2%).
  • Meanwhile, imports declined by 1.1% to €35B.

data.description:

data.title: Stock index futures up as hopes of peace deal between U.S. and Iran rise
data.description:
Stock index futures rose on Tuesday as renewed hopes for a peace deal between Iran and the U.S. lifted sentiment.

Dow futures (INDU) gained 0.12% to 49,504.18, while S&P 500 futures (SPX) advanced 0.16% to 7,120.82. Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) added 0.22% to 26,647.85.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday for another round of talks, CNN reported.

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.26%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) climbed 2 basis points to 3.75%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) ticked up 0.5 basis points to 4.89%.

Investors were preparing for economic data releases, including retail sales, business inventories, and the pending home sales index.

Top gainers in premarket trading included FirstEnergy (FE) +10.77%, Amazon (AMZN) +2.90%, and West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) +2.10%. Decliners included AutoZone (AZO) -4.36%, Live Nation Entertainment (LYV) -3.17%, and Ares Management (ARES) -1.28%.
